  <meta name="description" content="The Department of Mental Health (DMH) is dedicated to improving mental health for all individuals living in Massachusetts. At its core, DMH is an agency that wants to help people, and that is committed to promoting positive mental health and improving access to mental health care across the Commonwealth. DMH is particularly focused on equity and reaching communities that have historically been underserved.

DMH serves individuals and families across the state and has a specialized role in the healthcare delivery system. DMH supports promotion and prevention efforts and provides low barrier services available to everyone across Massachusetts. The Office of Behavioral Health Promotion & Prevention promotes wellness among all residents of the Commonwealth through education, outreach, and supporting philosophically aligned programs and initiatives across the state.

DMH also connects people to appropriate wellness services, including community resources. This is done through programs such as Young Adult Access Centers and Recovery Learning Communities which are free and available to everyone in Massachusetts. Additionally, DMH engages in awareness and education efforts, community outreach, early intervention, peer supports, mental health policy and regulation, licensure of private acute mental health facilities, as well as directly providing additional services, treatment, and supports.

For individuals with more serious needs, the Department provides a variety community-based and inpatient services which are not available elsewhere.

The Behavioral Health Help Line, which launched in 2023, connects people with services whether or not they have insurance, and whether they are seeking help for themselves or a loved one. Residents can call or text 833-773-2445 any time of the day or night, and in 200 languages. An option to chat with someone is also available by visiting www.masshelpline.com. | 11 comments on LinkedIn">

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">The Department of Mental Health (DMH) is dedicated to improving mental health for all individuals living in Massachusetts. At its core, DMH is an agency that wants to help people, and that is committed to promoting positive mental health and improving access to mental health care across the Commonwealth. DMH is particularly focused on equity and reaching communities that have historically been underserved.

DMH serves individuals and families across the state and has a specialized role in the healthcare delivery system. DMH supports promotion and prevention efforts and provides low barrier services available to everyone across Massachusetts. The Office of Behavioral Health Promotion & Prevention promotes wellness among all residents of the Commonwealth through education, outreach, and supporting philosophically aligned programs and initiatives across the state.

DMH also connects people to appropriate wellness services, including community resources. This is done through programs such as Young Adult Access Centers and Recovery Learning Communities which are free and available to everyone in Massachusetts. Additionally, DMH engages in awareness and education efforts, community outreach, early intervention, peer supports, mental health policy and regulation, licensure of private acute mental health facilities, as well as directly providing additional services, treatment, and supports.

For individuals with more serious needs, the Department provides a variety community-based and inpatient services which are not available elsewhere.

The Behavioral Health Help Line, which launched in 2023, connects people with services whether or not they have insurance, and whether they are seeking help for themselves or a loved one. Residents can call or text 833-773-2445 any time of the day or night, and in 200 languages. An option to chat with someone is also available by visiting <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww%2Emasshelpline%2Ecom&urlhash=d6Ln&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">www.masshelpline.com</a>.</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
         comment__text babybear:mt-0.5" dir="ltr">They have a discriminatory policy that needs to be adjusted. For those in the community in the severe spectrum of mental illness, they can only recieve DMH services if they willingly accept their services. If they are too ill to recognize their condition because of a common neurological  symptom called anosognosia, how can they self advocate? The whole point is to get them into the needed treatment so that they can then be well enough to stay dedicated to their prescribed medicine. Currently there is not much being done for the ones too ill to know they are ill, and this policy needs to change, and the AOT “Continuum of Care for Serious Mental Illness” S.2973 bill currently sitting in the statehouse needs to pass. <a class="link" href="http://www.AOTnow.org?trk=public_post_comment-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_comment-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">www.AOTnow.org</a>
#treatmentb4tragedy
#anosognosia
#psychosisisamedicalemergency</p>

    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
         comment__text babybear:mt-0.5" dir="ltr">Hey DMH….. a very loved and fragile DMH client age 38 was released Thursday from hospital care, and found dead in Lowell train station Sunday morning. He was Released with no effective discharge plan. He was told to go to a shelter, because there were no beds. He was treatment seeking.  He was denied a Sect 35 in Feb by the mental health court.   Is DMH ignoring the gravely ill?  We have a Bill in Ways and Means to stop this neglect.</p>

    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
         comment__text babybear:mt-0.5" dir="ltr">Sadly this week in Massachusetts we had a family lose their son, another who reached out with her story — she is a therapist who was stabbed 20+ times by her son who was experiencing psychosis and another because of the involvement of our organization was able to divert her daughter into treatment rather than jail and she is now doing well. While there are some excellent services in the state, there are large gaps for those who are gravely disabled. 99% of our state hospital beds are occupied by forensic patients as we continue to criminalize those with severe mental illness. <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerrymareemartin?trk=public_post_comment-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_comment-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Kerry Martin, MA Public Policy, Harvard</a> wrote a great paper on the standard of neglect in Massachusetts and calls for the right to be well. The suffering is immense and real solutions are needed. <a class="link" href="https://www.nationalshatteringsilencecoalition.org/uploads/1/4/4/2/144281710/nssc_frontlines_perspective__take_away_my_rights_if_i_lose_my_mind_again_by_kerry_martin.pdf?trk=public_post_comment-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_comment-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://www.nationalshatteringsilencecoalition.org/uploads/1/4/4/2/144281710/nssc_frontlines_perspective__take_away_my_rights_if_i_lose_my_mind_again_by_kerry_martin.pdf</a></p>
<!---->  </div>

         comment__text babybear:mt-0.5" dir="ltr">Lynda this is devastating. Thank you for advocating and raising awareness of the current Bill.   What happened to Mental Health Parity Law here in Massachusetts? Why are we going backward in service and treatment? Our Mental Health treatment system is in need of serious overhaul.   <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Today’s Government announcement on the development of a new cross-government Mental Health Strategy for England is an important opportunity to strengthen how people are supported much earlier, before they reach crisis point.

As Director of Operations at <a class="link" href="https://uk.linkedin.com/company/voiceability?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">VoiceAbility</a>, I welcome the focus on prevention, earlier intervention and the need to look beyond clinical services alone. Mental health is shaped by people’s experiences at home, in education, in work, in communities and across wider health and care systems.

For many people facing complex or fragmented systems, being heard is not always straightforward... 
Advocacy has an important role to play in this. It helps people understand their rights, express what matters to them, take part in decisions about their care and support, and challenge when things are not working as they should.
Any future Mental Health Strategy must be shaped by the voices of people with lived experience, including those who are least likely to be heard through traditional consultation routes. Prevention must include listening earlier, responding proportionately, and making sure people can influence the decisions that affect their lives.

At VoiceAbility, we look forward to engaging with the call for evidence and working with partners to help ensure the strategy reflects the experiences, rights and voices of the people who need support most.

</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">We welcome the Government’s commitment to establishing a cross-government mental health strategy. 

This is an important first step in taking a joined up approach to addressing systemic inequalities in mental health and access to support.  

Over the past two decades, psychological distress has been on the rise 📈 

Young people today face greater mental health problems than ever before. 

Yet many young people face barriers to accessing support when they need it.

Pushed from one service to another or left on waiting lists while their mental health deteriorates. 

This strategy presents an opportunity to build services that centre the needs of young people, rather than the needs of the system. 

That means listening to young people and designing it with them. 

This means scaling up what we know already works, rather than starting again from scratch 👇

Our members, a network of early support hubs up and down the country, have transformed the lives of young people for decades.

We look forward to sharing their expertise and insight as this strategy takes shape and working to ensure it delivers lasting change for young people.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">A welcome and much-needed step from government: a new national mental health strategy focused on prevention, earlier support, and listening to lived experience.

For those of us working with children, young people and families, we see every day how early intervention can change trajectories — not just outcomes in crisis.

The challenge now is turning ambition into accessible, joined-up support in communities, schools and services where people actually need it.

Encouraging to see a call for evidence that invites voices from frontline practice and lived experience alike.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">A new report from Inseparable, a nonpartisan mental health policy organization, maps the gap between mental health need and available care across every state.

Pennslyvania currently meets just 32.1% of its need for psychiatrists. Pennsylvanians seeking mental health care are going out of network 5.2 times more often than those seeking physical health care. Out-of-network care typically means higher costs and greater barriers to follow-through. The report categorizes Pennsylvania's community mental health provider supply as facing a moderate shortage, and its crisis systems as facing a catastrophic one.

These gaps carry consequences beyond missed appointments. The report notes that when timely care is out of reach, the effects ripple across families and communities, contributing to housing instability, avoidable disability, and added strain on emergency rooms, schools, and law enforcement.

For families already navigating financial precarity, a mental health crisis can be the event that makes keeping a job, paying rent, or managing a household untenable. Strengthening the mental health workforce is, among other things, a housing stability issue.

Read the full report: <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeT_eynWA&urlhash=GDVs&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/eT_eynWA</a></p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Mental health challenges continue to impact individuals and families across the metro-Denver community, yet access to care remains a major barrier. Cost, lack of insurance, and limited availability of culturally responsive providers often prevent people from seeking the support they <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http%3A%2F%2Fneed%2EIn&urlhash=NHSh&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">need.In</a> Colorado, thousands of individuals go without mental health services each year, with disparities disproportionately affecting Black communities due to longstanding systemic inequities. Without access to affordable and trusted care, many are left to navigate challenges alone—delaying support and worsening outcomes.The Center for African American Health is addressing this gap through its Behavioral Health Assistance Program, which connects community members to therapy services. This program removes financial barriers while prioritizing access to culturally responsive care that meets individuals where they are.Through this initiative, participants are matched with licensed therapists and supported in navigating the process of getting connected to care. By simplifying access and centering community needs, CAA Health is helping individuals take the first step toward healing and long-term wellness.Taking the first step toward mental wellness should not come with barriers. To learn more and to get connected to therapy services, please contact <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=mailto%3Akourtlin%40caahealth%2EorgShare&urlhash=jxoo&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">kourtlin@caahealth.orgShare</a> this resource with someone who may benefit or partner with us to expand access. Together, we can ensure that mental health care is accessible, equitable, and community-centered.</p>

    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Positive news from <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/company/access-wellbeing-dorset?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Access Wellbeing Dorset</a> who have announced that a new single point of access (SPoA) will be introduced for referrals to Dorset’s adult community mental health services from primary care.

This new system is being introduced as part of the wider Access Wellbeing transformation programme, bringing together specialist mental health support (CMHTs), focused pathways (including complex trauma and medication management) and universal community-based support into one single, joined-up model. The SPoA will provide a route into support across the whole system.

Find out more: <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeSe688XM&urlhash=H3Oj&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/eSe688XM</a>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Mental health challenges continue to impact individuals and families across the metro-Denver community, yet access to care remains a major barrier. Cost, lack of insurance, and limited availability of culturally responsive providers often prevent people from seeking the support they need.

In Colorado, thousands of individuals go without mental health services each year, with disparities disproportionately affecting Black communities due to longstanding systemic inequities. Without access to affordable and trusted care, many are left to navigate challenges alone—delaying support and worsening outcomes.

The Center for African American Health is addressing this gap through its Behavioral Health Assistance Program, which connects community members to therapy services. This program removes financial barriers while prioritizing access to culturally responsive care that meets individuals where they are.

Through this initiative, participants are matched with licensed therapists and supported in navigating the process of getting connected to care. By simplifying access and centering community needs, CAA Health is helping individuals take the first step toward healing and long-term wellness.

Taking the first step toward mental wellness should not come with barriers. To learn more and to get connected to therapy services, please contact <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=mailto%3Akourtlin%40caahealth%2Eorg&urlhash=Esbt&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">kourtlin@caahealth.org</a>

Share this resource with someone who may benefit or partner with us to expand access. Together, we can ensure that mental health care is accessible, equitable, and community-centered.</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Access is still one of the biggest issues in behavioral health.

According to the National Council for Mental Wellbeing’s 2022 Access to Care Survey, 43% of U.S. adults who said they needed mental health or substance use care in the past year did not receive it. The survey also found that many people faced barriers like cost, insurance issues, difficulty finding providers, visit format limitations, and lack of immediate appointment availability.  

That should get our attention.

Because when someone is finally ready to ask for help, the next step should not feel impossible.

Families should not have to call ten places just to find out who answers.
Hospitals should not struggle to find a next level of care.
Employers and EAP teams should not be left without clear referral options.
Courts and community partners should not have to guess where to send someone.

Substance use disorder treatment is not just about whether help exists.

It is about whether people can actually reach it when they need it.

The survey also found that 28% of people who did not receive needed substance use care said they could not get an appointment immediately when they needed care.  

That matters because the window of willingness can be small.

If someone says, “I need help,” we need systems that respond quickly, clearly, and compassionately.

Better access is not just a healthcare goal.

It is a recovery issue.
It is a family issue.
It is a workforce issue.
It is a community issue.

For those working in behavioral health, hospitals, HR, EAP, courts, case management, and community outreach:

What do you believe is the biggest barrier to care right now: cost, insurance, provider availability, transportation, stigma, or lack of immediate placement options?

Drop your thoughts in the comments. I would really like to hear your perspective.
